Press enter or click to view image in full size

Like negotiations, waves emerge from countless interacting forces, each one following patterns, yet each one unique. (Image by Ivan Bandura)

6 min read
Just now

--

The vendor claim changed this year and most of the market has not noticed. For a decade the autonomous negotiations pitch was tooling: systems that run negotiation events faster, more consistently, at scale. On autopilot. For spend which humans do not have the time to cover it themselves. The new pitch is labor. Leading funded vendors now sell an AI workforce for procurement, complete with named digital colleagues, and in many versions of that pitch the flagship capability is the same one this series has examined from the start. Negotiation.
That is not an accident. Nobody needs a named persona to believe invoice matching is automatable. The workforce framing only earns the word workforce if the system can do the thing the market still treats as the human core of the function: read a supplier situation, design a strategy, and negotiate it. Negotiation is where the substitution claim either proves itself or collapses into operations automation wearing a name badge.
Most responses to the claim fall into two useless categories. Cheerleading, usually from people selling the substitution. And reassurance, usually from people selling the opposite. Both skip the step procurement exists to perform: evaluating a consequential vendor claim on its merits.
You are now the buyer of your own replacement. Nobody is better qualified to run that evaluation, and almost nobody is doing it.
The entire workforce claim is a bet on one layer
Readers of this series know its central distinction: autonomous negotiations is not one capability but two. An execution track, the negotiation event runtime of bids, counteroffers, and closure. And an intelligence track, everything that happens before the first offer: strategy, market read, negotiation design, the mandate that defines what a good outcome even is.
Decompose the AI workforce pitch against that taxonomy and the bundle gets thin quickly.

• The operational layer. Intake, matching, exception handling. This was conceded to automation years ago. Demonstrated performance here proves nothing about negotiation. It is filler in the SKU.

• The execution track. Real and established. This series has covered its results and its boundaries at length. But execution autonomy has existed commercially for years without anyone calling it a workforce.

• The intelligence track. This is the new assertion in the pitch. The system does not just run the event. It decides what the event should be.
The premium in the price sits entirely on that last claim. The evaluation that follows is therefore not a general automation assessment. It is a negotiation capability assessment, because that is the only layer where the claim is doing any work.
The bottom line: strip the bundle and the AI workforce claim reduces to a single proposition. The two tracks have merged into one employable unit. That is precisely the proposition this series has argued the market keeps getting wrong.
Concede what the execution evidence has already proven
Any credible evaluation starts by conceding the strongest part of the counterparty’s case, and on the execution track the case is strong.

• The savings are real. One European telecom procurement arm reported average savings of 5.2 percent on autonomous negotiation events, delivered in minutes rather than weeks. I have seen comparable results in programmes I have worked on directly.

• The throughput argument is over. A system running hundreds of parallel negotiations on structured, price-led spend beats any human team on coverage, cycle time, and consistency. Holding that territory was never a survival strategy.

• The market has internalised it. An executive survey found 53 percent of executives expect AI agents to negotiate customer or supplier deals within twelve months, and 87 percent within three years.
If your time today is spent executing negotiation events on tactical spend, that work sits on the substitutable side of the line. The honest response is to plan accordingly, not to argue with the data.
This means: the contested question is not whether the system can negotiate. It is whether the system can decide what to negotiate, against which mandate, and know when its own strategy is wrong. That is the intelligence track, and here the evidence gets genuinely uncomfortable. In both directions.
The evidence against the human
If this post stopped at the previous section it would be the comfortable version, and the comfortable version is dishonest. Because the strongest research findings do not say the intelligence track is safe human territory. They say humans systematically overestimate their marginal contribution to negotiation outcomes.

• Human-AI teams lose on decision tasks. Vaccaro and colleagues, in a meta-analysis of 106 experiments published in Nature Human Behaviour, found that human-AI combinations on average performed worse than the best of the human or the AI alone on decision-making tasks. The combination gains appeared in creation tasks. Negotiation strategy is a decision task.

• Tactical input adds nothing measurable. A 2025 controlled study of frontier-model negotiations found that tactical instructions from humans had no statistically significant effect on outcomes.

• The losses are invisible to the losers. The same study found the losing side generally could not detect that it had lost. Research on capability-mismatched agent negotiations shows weaker agents losing up to 14 percent of achievable profit, with neither side’s human principals positioned to see it.
Read together, the implication is sharp. The human in the negotiation loop is not automatically a value-add. In decision tasks the human can be a cost center wearing a control function’s badge, adding latency and variance while believing they are adding judgment.
A disclosure you should weigh while reading this: I work at a vendor whose product can keeps the buyer in the loop. My employer’s commercial position benefits if you conclude the human is irreplaceable. That is exactly why I am putting the Vaccaro finding in front of you rather than hoping you do not find it. I have built on both sides of this divide, and the pattern is the same on both: every party in this market, including mine, has an incentive to overstate the value of whichever component it sells. Discount all of us accordingly.
What actually survives the evaluation
Strip away the sentiment and three things hold up under the evidence, none of which is negotiation instinct in the vague sense the reassurance genre uses.

• Mandate authority. Someone decides what outcome the system is allowed to pursue, against which constraints, at which risk tolerance. The research showing humans add little tactical value says nothing about mandate-setting, because mandate-setting happens before the game starts. Every finding above assumes a mandate already existed. No study has shown a system generating its own mandate and being held to it.

• Accountability that can be sanctioned. When a negotiated outcome is wrong, the organisation needs a party that can be held responsible in a way that changes future behaviour. A model cannot be demoted or deposed. This is an institutional constraint, not a capability one, and institutions move slower than benchmarks.

• Evaluation of the negotiating systems themselves. The capability-mismatch research carries a second message the workforce vendors do not advertise: when agents negotiate against agents, the weaker system bleeds value invisibly. Someone has to assess which side of that mismatch your organisation is on, continuously, as capabilities shift. That skill is the least represented in any vendor demo.
Notice what is not on this list: relationship warmth, reading the room, deal instinct. Not because they are worthless, but because the evidence that they outperform systematic alternatives is weaker than the people who possess them believe.
The bottom line: a due diligence process does not credit unevidenced assets. Yours should not either, no matter whose assets they are.
The checklist, since this is a due diligence post

• Track-separated evidence. Execution results and intelligence results, unbundled. No transferring proof from event runtime to strategy capability.

• The counterfactual. Every savings figure gets asked: against what baseline, negotiated on what mandate, and who wrote that mandate?

• The mandate author. If every showcased result had its mandate written by a senior category manager, the workforce claim just conceded its own core.

• A blind pilot of the intelligence track. If the system’s negotiation strategy cannot beat your current category manager’s under equal information, you are buying execution capability at a workforce price.

• The same tests on your own hybrid setup. If your human review step cannot demonstrate outcome improvement over the system negotiating alone, you have learned something you needed to know. Whatever you decide to do with it.
The professionals who run this evaluation honestly will end up owning it. That is the version of survival the evidence actually supports: not the negotiator the system could not replace, but the person the organisation trusts to decide which negotiations get delegated, on what mandate, and how anyone would know if the system is quietly losing.
Over to you
I have made concessions in this post that people on my side of the market usually avoid, and I have probably still not been hard enough on my own position. If you have evaluated a workforce-substitution claim against real negotiation outcomes, or if you think the three survivors in section 4 do not survive either, I want to hear it. The comments are open.
